scope: Latin-American family-law practitioners (BR/MX/AR/CO/CL) who have appeared as named counsel / promovente in apex PA litigation, published in the field at scholarly / book-length level, chaired an official PA-related expert body, or publicly stated a sourced stance through institutional advocacy. Institutional entries listed where the institution itself has filed a court action / issued a sourced PA position and named individual members are confirmable.

stance_taxonomy.recognition: Treats parental alienation as a legitimate, litigable phenomenon
stance_taxonomy.critique: Argues PA / SAP is misused in family courts, especially against DV survivors; supports statutory repeal or proscription
stance_taxonomy.middle: Procedural-reform / differentiated approach engaging both DV and alienating-behaviour frames
stance_taxonomy.not_publicly_confirmed: Included for record-completeness; PA-specific stance not in primary sources

honest_gaps: Apex-court counsel anonymisation: BR (STJ), AR (CSJN, SCBA), CO (Corte Constitucional T-526/23 tutela) anonymise child parties and frequently their counsel. Named-counsel entries are concentrated on (a) constitutional-action promoventes where standing is institutional and (b) doctrinal-commentary authors.
Chile sparsity: Chile lacks single anchor apex-court PA ruling comparable to MX 11/2016, CO T-526/23, AR P.B., E.G. Both flagship CL entries are partial-fit rather than perfect-fit.

PUC Chile / Centro UC de la Familia (Carmen Domínguez): substantial family-law output but PA-specific publication not surfaced in available web sources. Spanish-language academic-library pass would likely identify a PA piece.

Critique-camp named-counsel verification: original brief's hypothesis of a single named Brazilian advocate fronting Alsalem 2022 submission appears to overstate institutional reality. The Alsalem call drew on AAIG/Themis collective work plus Câmara feminist deputies (Melchionna, Bomfim, Reis on PL 2812/22) — not a single named advocate.

Mexico CDMX private practice: PA jurisprudence dominated by institutional / constitutional-action lawyers. Private-practice family lawyers handling individual SCJN amparo cases not retrievable from public-source searches at directory level.
